message.content:
Crypto bug-bounty lead reporting to Coinbase cb-mpc. Findings below survived a 3-lens adversarial panel. Produce a bounty-aware ranked report.

BOUNTY SCOPE: eligible only if reachable via public APIs in include/cbmpc/api/. demo-* and include/cbmpc/c_api/* are OUT OF SCOPE as entry points. Primitive/ZK bugs eligible only when tied to a named public-API path. Medium+ needs an independent-parties PoC (>=1 honest party on unmodified code; malicious party interacts only through the public protocol boundary). Tiers: Critical $50k (key compromise/RCE), High $15k, Medium $2k, Low $200.

Map each to a tier + est_reward; set poc_required=true for Medium+ and describe the minimal independent-parties PoC via include/cbmpc/api/ only. Be conservative; if empty, record the null result and name the deepest-uncertainty spots. coverage_and_caveats required.

FINDINGS JSON:
[
  {
    "title": "Batch PVE decrypt: attacker-controlled inner Q vector length is not re-checked against batch_count, causing out-of-bounds std::vector read of ecc_point_t (wild EC_POINT* deref) on the honest decryptor",
    "public_api_entry": "coinbase::api::pve::decrypt_batch(curve_id, dk, ek, ciphertext, label, out_xs) in include/cbmpc/api/pve_batch_single_recipient.h",
    "file": "src/cbmpc/protocol/pve_batch.cpp",
    "line": 183,
    "vuln_class": "Out-of-bounds read / missing length validation on deserialized container",
    "severity": "Medium",
    "missing_check": "After deserializing ec_pve_batch_t from the untrusted blob, nothing verifies that the inner length-prefixed Q vector (read by ec_pve_batch_t::convert -> converter.convert(Q,...)) has size == n. ec_pve_batch_t::convert (include-internal/cbmpc/internal/protocol/pve_batch.h:48-61) checks `if (int(Q.size()) != n)` BEFORE reading, when Q is still the constructor-sized vector, so the check is a no-op; the subsequent std::vector overload does Q.clear()/resize(attacker_count), making Q.size() fully attacker-controlled. verify() (pve_batch.cpp:78) does enforce Q.size()==n, but the decrypt path runs with skip_verify=true and never re-validates. restore_from_decrypted() validates row_index but not Q.size().",
    "data_flow": "decrypt_batch -> parse_batch_ciphertext sets n = blob.batch_count (separate outer field, bounded by MAX_BATCH_COUNT) -> ec_pve_batch_t pve_ct(n) (constructor resizes Q to n) -> coinbase::convert(pve_ct, blob.ct) -> ec_pve_batch_t::convert: pre-check Q.size()==n passes (still n), then converter.convert(Q,...) clears and resizes Q to the attacker's inner length prefix m (m can be < n) -> get_Qs() curve loop iterates only the m present entries (no rejection) -> pve_ct.decrypt(..., skip_verify=true) -> for each kappa row, base_pke.decrypt(dk, inner_label, rows[i].c, x_buf): attacker can craft rows[i].c as a valid base-PKE ciphertext under the public ek so this returns SUCCESS for some row -> restore_from_decrypted(i, x_buf, curve, xs): builds x0,x1 of size n via bn_t::vector_from_bin, then loop `for (int i=0;i<n;i++){ x[i]=x0[i]+x1[i]; if (Q[i] != x[i]*G) ... }` reads Q[i] for i up to n-1 while Q.size()==m<n -> OOB std::vector<ecc_point_t> element whose embedded EC_POINT* pointer is uninitialized/out-of-bounds heap memory, then dereferenced inside ecc_point_t::operator!= (OpenSSL EC_POINT_cmp).",
    "impact": "A malicious counterparty who supplies the serialized batch-PVE ciphertext can force the honest party's decrypt_batch (or get_public_keys_compressed_batch / decrypt_batch_*_hsm, which share the same construct+deserialize pattern) to read past the end of the Q vector and dereference a wild EC_POINT* in OpenSSL. At minimum this is a remotely triggerable crash (DoS) of the honest decryptor; because the wild pointer is consumed as an OpenSSL EC_POINT object, the concrete impact ranges from segfault to controlled OOB read depending on heap layout. No honest-party secret is leaked, but it is reachable purely through the public API boundary with valid base-PKE rows.",
    "poc_sketch": "1) Honest party generates (ek,dk) via generate_base_pke_*_keypair and publishes ek. 2) Attacker calls encrypt_batch(curve, ek, label, xs) with xs of size m (e.g. m=1) to obtain a well-formed pve_batch_ciphertext_blob_v1_t whose inner ct serializes a valid ec_pve_batch_t with Q.size()==1 and valid rows[i].c under ek. 3) Attacker edits only the OUTER blob field batch_count from 1 to a larger n (e.g. 50000), leaving the inner ct (Q vector length prefix = 1, valid c rows) unchanged; re-serialize. 4) Honest party calls decrypt_batch(curve, dk, ek, attacker_blob, label, out_xs). parse sets n=50000; constructor sizes Q to 50000; deserialization overwrites Q with the 1-element inner vector; skip_verify decrypt reaches restore_from_decrypted which loops i in [0,50000) reading Q[i] far past the 1 valid element -> OOB read / crash. Honest party runs unmodified code; attacker only crosses the public ciphertext boundary.",
    "votes": "3/3",
    "verifier_notes": [
      {
        "real": true,
        "refuted": false,
        "reachable": true,
        "check_present": false,
        "intended": false,
        "attack": "Malicious counterparty supplies the serialized batch-PVE ciphertext blob; honest party calls the public API coinbase::api::pve::decrypt_batch (include/cbmpc/api/pve_batch_single_recipient.h). The malicious party interacts only through the public API boundary, consistent with BUG_BOUNTY.md scope (include/cbmpc/api/).",
        "reason": "Verified against the real code. (1) Deserialization no-op pre-check confirmed: ec_pve_batch_t::convert (include-internal/.../pve_batch.h:48-61) checks `if (int(Q.size()) != n)` while Q is still constructor-sized n (passes), THEN calls converter.convert(Q,L,b). The std::vector overload in convert.h:114-131 does value.clear() then value.resize(count) where count is read from the wire (capped only by MAX_CONTAINER_ELEMENTS=2^20, so attacker can pick m<n). So Q.size() becomes attacker-controlled m, decoupled from the n member. (2) Public-API reachability confirmed: decrypt_batch (src/.../pve_batch_single_recipient.cpp:139) sets n=blob.batch_count, builds pve_ct(n), runs coinbase::convert(pve_ct, blob.ct) which uses the single-arg convert (convert.h:276) that does NOT enforce no-trailing-bytes, then calls pve_ct.decrypt(..., skip_verify=true). The only loop over Q (lines 165-167) checks curve, not size. (3) verify() (pve_batch.cpp:74-78,86) DOES enforce size via `Q.size()!=n` and `Q!=this->Q`, but the decrypt path bypasses verify (skip_verify=true) and never re-checks Q.size(). restore_from_decrypted (pve_batch.cpp:144-184) validates row_index and buffer sizes but never Q.size(); its loop `for(i=0;i<n;i++){...; if(Q[i]!=x[i]*G)...}` indexes Q (size m) up to n-1 via unchecked operator[], an OOB std::vector<ecc_point_t> read. (4) Trigger feasibility confirmed: base PKE is standard KEM/DEM public-key crypto (pve_base.h:50-82); ek is public and supplied by the attacker, so the attacker encrypts chosen plaintexts that the honest dk decrypts back exactly, controlling x_buf size/content; inner_label=genPVELabelWithPoint(label,Q) (pve_base.h:254) is computable by the attacker. With m=0 (inner Q length prefix 0, n>=1) the OOB occurs on the very first iteration Q[0] as soon as any row decrypts to an n*curve_size buffer — no equality needs to be satisfied. ecc_point_t OOB element interprets adjacent heap as {ecurve_t curve; EC_POINT* ptr;} and operator!= -> operator== (base_ecc.cpp:909-917) derefs curve.ptr->equ_points when the bytes are non-null, i.e., wild EC_POINT* into OpenSSL. Impact: remotely-triggerable crash/DoS of the honest decryptor, possibly controlled OOB read depending on heap layout; no key-material disclosure. Same construct+deserialize-without-size-recheck pattern is shared by get_public_keys_compressed_batch and get_Label_batch (which iterate only the m present entries, so those are non-OOB but show the missing-validation pattern). Confidence is high but not maximal because a precise PoC must construct a fully self-consistent serialized blob (correct length prefixes for the 128 rows, a base-PKE ciphertext under the real RSA/ECIES KEM that decrypts to the right-sized buffer, and matching inner_label); these are all attacker-constructible but not yet demonstrated end-to-end here."
      },
      {
        "real": true,
        "refuted": false,
        "reachable": true,
        "check_present": false,
        "intended": false,
        "attack": "Malicious counterparty supplies the serialized batch-PVE ciphertext blob consumed by the honest decryptor through the public API coinbase::api::pve::decrypt_batch(...) (and decrypt_batch_rsa_oaep_hsm / decrypt_batch_ecies_p256_hsm, which call into the same path). The honest party holds dk; the attacker only needs the public ek.",
        "reason": "Verified against pristine upstream cb-mpc (commit e97eec7, clean working tree, origin coinbase/cb-mpc).\n\nCORE CLAIM CONFIRMED. The inner Q vector length (m) is decoupled from the outer batch_count (n) and is never re-checked on the decrypt path:\n\n1) Outer n is fixed by the constructor: decrypt_batch sets n = blob.batch_count (bounded 1..MAX_BATCH_COUNT=100000) and constructs ec_pve_batch_t pve_ct(n), whose ctor does Q.resize(n) and stores this->n = n. Deserialization never touches this->n.\n\n2) The pre-check is a genuine no-op (pve_batch.h:48-52). On deserialization Q.size()==n at entry (ctor-sized), so `if (int(Q.size()) != n)` passes, THEN `converter.convert(Q, L, b)` runs. The generic std::vector<T> overload (convert.h:114-131) does value.clear(); reads attacker length-prefix count=m; only checks count > MAX_CONTAINER_ELEMENTS (1<<20); then value.resize(m). So Q.size() becomes attacker-controlled m, with 0 <= m < n fully achievable. coinbase::convert(T&,mem_t) (convert.h:276-282) does NOT enforce trailing-byte/offset==size, so a short Q deserializes cleanly.\n\n3) No re-validation on the decrypt path. The only Q.size()==n check in the codebase is verify() at pve_batch.cpp:78, and decrypt_batch calls decrypt(..., skip_verify=true) so verify is skipped. The post-deserialize loop `for (auto& q : pve_ct.get_Qs())` (pve_batch_single_recipient.cpp:165-167) iterates only the m present entries (curve check only) — no count check. restore_from_decrypted (pve_batch.cpp:144-187) validates only row_index and buffer sizes (against n*curve_size), never Q.size().\n\n4) OOB read is reachable. restore_from_decrypted loops `for (int i=0;i<n;i++){ x[i]=x0[i]+x1[i]; if (Q[i] != x[i]*G) return E_CRYPTO; }` (pve_batch.cpp:181-184). x0 (DRBG, size n) and x1 (vector_from_bin requires x1_bin.size==n*curve_size -> size n; base_bn.cpp:536-549) are both size n. The attacker, as ciphertext producer, fully controls Q (m points), b (selects bi per row), row.r and row.c. Choosing bi=0 makes x1_bin the base-PKE plaintext of row.c — and since ek is public, the attacker encrypts a chosen n*curve_size plaintext under ek so base_pke.decrypt succeeds and yields that plaintext. They then set Q[i]=x[i]*G for i in [0,m), so the equality holds for the first m iterations and the loop advances to i=m, reading Q[m] past the end of a std::vector<ecc_point_t> of size m. The OOB-read element's embedded curve/EC_POINT* pointers are then consumed by ecc_point_t::operator!= -> operator== (base_ecc.cpp:909-917): `if(!ptr) return val.ptr==nullptr; if(!curve) return false; return curve.ptr->equ_points(...)` -> deref of out-of-bounds-read pointers (wild EC_POINT* / curve vtable).\n\n5) Not intended design. The header doc disclaims only secret-leakage (\"invalid ciphertexts ... designed to not leak secret information\"), not memory safety; an OOB read/wild-pointer deref is outside that disclaimer.\n\nImpact: remotely triggerable OOB read and wild-pointer dereference in the honest decryptor reachable through the public API with a valid base-PKE row; at minimum a DoS crash, with potential for controlled OOB depending on heap layout. No honest-party secret is disclosed. Medium is appropriate.\n\nMinor inaccuracy in candidate (does not affect the core finding): the cited get_public_keys_compressed_batch path is NOT OOB — it iterates the m-sized Q vector directly (pve_batch_single_recipient.cpp:265) and does not loop to n. Only the decrypt path (and the HSM decrypt variants delegating to it) hits the n-bounded loop."
      },
      {
        "real": true,
        "refuted": false,
        "reachable": true,
        "check_present": false,
        "intended": false,
        "attack": "Deserialization trust boundary: coinbase::api::pve::decrypt_batch (and decrypt_batch_rsa_oaep_hsm / decrypt_batch_ecies_p256_hsm) consuming the untrusted serialized pve_batch ciphertext blob supplied by a malicious counterparty. The malicious-party deviation: serialize ec_pve_batch_t with an inner Q length-prefix m strictly less than the outer batch_count n, with all 128 rows present and x_bin sized n*curve_size, and at least one row.c a valid base-PKE ciphertext under the public ek and inner_label=sha256(serialized m-point Q). The honest decryptor (skip_verify=true) deserializes Q to size m, decrypts that row, enters restore_from_decrypted, and reads Q[i] for i up to n-1 (OOB), dereferencing a wild EC_POINT* inside ecc_point_t::operator!=.",
        "reason": "Verified against the real cb-mpc code; every load-bearing claim holds.\n\nROOT CAUSE (confirmed): converter_t's std::vector overload (include-internal/cbmpc/internal/core/convert.h:114-131) does value.clear() then value.resize(count) using the attacker's length prefix (bounded only by MAX_CONTAINER_ELEMENTS=1<<20). In ec_pve_batch_t::convert (include-internal/cbmpc/internal/protocol/pve_batch.h:48-61) the guard `if (int(Q.size()) != n)` runs BEFORE converter.convert(Q,L,b) while Q is still constructor-sized n (pve_batch.h:13 Q.resize(n)). So the guard is a genuine no-op, and convert() then sets Q.size() = attacker count m. The outer loop bound n stays = blob.batch_count, a SEPARATE field (pve_batch_single_recipient.cpp:159,162), decoupled from Q.size().\n\nBYPASS OF EXISTING CHECKS (confirmed): the only Q.size()==n enforcement is in verify() (pve_batch.cpp:78), but decrypt_batch calls decrypt with skip_verify=true (pve_batch_single_recipient.cpp:173-174 -> pve_batch.cpp:193 short-circuits verify). The post-deser curve loop (pve_batch_single_recipient.cpp:165-167) only iterates the m present get_Qs() entries, so it does not detect m<n. coinbase::convert(pve_ct, blob.ct) (convert.h:276-282) does NOT enforce the strict trailing-byte check that deser() has, and the blob is fully attacker-constructible anyway (length-prefix m, m points, L, b, then kappa=128 fixed rows).\n\nOOB + DEREF (confirmed): restore_from_decrypted (pve_batch.cpp:181-184) loops for(i=0;i<n;i++) and reads Q[i] while Q.size()==m<n. x0/x1 (bn_t::vector_from_bin resizes to n, base_bn.cpp:547) and xs (resize(n)) are all size n and safe; only Q is short, so Q[i] is an unchecked std::vector::operator[] OOB read of an ecc_point_t. ecc_point_t::operator!= -> operator== (base_ecc.cpp:909-917) reads this->ptr/this->curve from the OOB struct and calls curve.ptr->equ_points(...), dereferencing a wild EC_POINT*/curve pointer.\n\nREACHABILITY (confirmed achievable): reaching restore_from_decrypted requires base_pke.decrypt to return SUCCESS for >=1 of the 128 rows (pve_batch.cpp:200). The base PKE is RSA-OAEP-2048 / ECIES-P256 (pve_base_pke.cpp); the malicious party holds the PUBLIC ek and controls both Q and label, so it can compute inner_label=genPVELabelWithPoint(label,Q) (sha256 of attacker's m-point Q) and forge a validly-decrypting row. No stdlib hardening turns operator[] into a checked access (compilation_flags.cmake: no _GLIBCXX_ASSERTIONS/libc++ hardening; _FORTIFY_SOURCE=2 does not cover vector::operator[]), and the c_api catch(...) (c_api/pve_batch_single_recipient.cpp) does not catch a SIGSEGV.\n\nIMPACT: a remotely-triggerable OOB read + wild EC_POINT* dereference on the honest decryptor, reachable purely through the public decrypt_batch / decrypt_batch_rsa_oaep_hsm / decrypt_batch_ecies_p256_hsm API by a malicious counterparty supplying the ciphertext blob. Crash/DoS is certain; controlled OOB read is plausible depending on heap layout. No honest secret is leaked.\n\nMINOR OVERSTATEMENT (does not refute): the candidate lists get_public_keys_compressed_batch / get_Label_batch as equally vulnerable. They are NOT — they only iterate the m-sized get_Qs() and never index to n, so they cannot trigger the OOB. Only the decrypt_batch family is exploitable. Core finding stands."
      }
    ]
  }
]

attachment.content:
- deep-research: Deep research harness — fan-out web searches, fetch sources, adversarially verify claims, synthesize a cited report. - When the user wants a deep, multi-source, fact-checked research report on any topic. BEFORE invoking, check if the question is specific enough to research directly — if underspecified (e.g., "what car to buy" without budget/use-case/region), ask 2-3 clarifying questions to narrow scope. Then pass the refined question as args, weaving the answers in.
- contribute:contribute: This skill should be used when the user asks to "contribute to open source", "find an issue to work on", "analyze a repo", "submit a pull request", "review PR feedback", "triage an issue", "sync my fork", "create a release", "clean up contribution branches", "debug CI failures", or mentions open-source contribution workflows. Provides a full-lifecycle open-source contribution system with 12 phases.
- update-config: Use this skill to configure the Claude Code harness via settings.json. Automated behaviors ("from now on when X", "each time X", "whenever X", "before/after X") require hooks configured in settings.json - the harness executes these, not Claude, so memory/preferences cannot fulfill them. Also use for: permissions ("allow X", "add permission", "move permission to"), env vars ("set X=Y"), hook troubleshooting, or any changes to settings.json/settings.local.json files. Examples: "allow npm commands", "add bq permission to global settings", "move permission to user settings", "set DEBUG=true", "when claude stops show X". For simple settings like theme/model, suggest the /config command.
- keybindings-help: Use when the user wants to customize keyboard shortcuts, rebind keys, add chord bindings, or modify ~/.claude/keybindings.json. Examples: "rebind ctrl+s", "add a chord shortcut", "change the submit key", "customize keybindings".
- verify: Verify that a code change actually does what it's supposed to by running the app and observing behavior. Use when asked to verify a PR, confirm a fix works, test a change manually, check that a feature works, or validate local changes before pushing.
- code-review: Review the current diff for correctness bugs and reuse/simplification/efficiency cleanups at the given effort level (low/medium: fewer, high-confidence findings; high→max: broader coverage, may include uncertain findings). Pass --comment to post findings as inline PR comments, or --fix to apply the findings to the working tree after the review.
- simplify: Review the changed code for reuse, simplification, efficiency, and altitude cleanups, then apply the fixes. Quality only — it does not hunt for bugs; use /code-review for that.
- fewer-permission-prompts: Scan your transcripts for common read-only Bash and MCP tool calls, then add a prioritized allowlist to project .claude/settings.json to reduce permission prompts.
- loop: Run a prompt or slash command on a recurring interval (e.g. /loop 5m /foo). Omit the interval to let the model self-pace. - When the user wants to set up a recurring task, poll for status, or run something repeatedly on an interval (e.g. "check the deploy every 5 minutes", "keep running /babysit-prs"). Do NOT invoke for one-off tasks.
- schedule: Create, update, list, or run scheduled cloud agents (routines) that execute on a cron schedule. - When the user wants to schedule a recurring cloud agent, set up automated tasks, create a cron job for Claude Code, or manage their scheduled agents/routines. Also use when the user wants a one-time scheduled run ("run this once at 3pm", "remind me to check X tomorrow").
- claude-api: Reference for the Claude API / Anthropic SDK — model ids, pricing, params, streaming, tool use, MCP, agents, caching, token counting, model migration.
TRIGGER — read BEFORE opening the target file; don't skip because it "looks like a one-liner" — whenever: the prompt names Claude/Anthropic in any form (Claude, Anthropic, Fable, Opus, Sonnet, Haiku, `anthropic`, `@anthropic-ai`, `claude-*`, `us.anthropic.*`, `[1m]`); the user asks about an LLM (pricing/model choice/limits/caching) — never answer from memory; OR the task is LLM-shaped with provider unstated (agent/MCP/tool-definition/multi-agent/RAG/LLM-judge/computer-use; generate/summarize/extract/classify/rewrite/converse over NL; debugging refusals/cutoffs/streaming/tool-calls/tokens).
SKIP only when another provider is being worked on (overrides all triggers): OpenAI/GPT/Gemini/Llama/Mistral/Cohere/Ollama named in the query; OR `grep -rE 'openai|langchain_openai|google.generativeai|genai|mistralai|cohere|ollama'` over the project hits (run this grep FIRST if no provider named — don't Read the file).
- run: Launch and drive this project's app to see a change working. Use when asked to run, start, or screenshot the app, or to confirm a change works in the real app (not just tests). First looks for a project skill that already covers launching the app; otherwise falls back to built-in patterns per project type (CLI, server, TUI, Electron, browser-driven, library).
- init: Initialize a new CLAUDE.md file with codebase documentation
- review: Review a pull request
- security-review: Complete a security review of the pending changes on the current branch

content.text: I'll verify this finding against the actual cb-mpc source code before producing the bounty report. Let me start by locating the relevant files.

input.command: find /c/bugBounty -type d -name "cb-mpc" 2>/dev/null; echo "---"; find /c/bugBounty -path "*api/pve_batch*" 2>/dev/null; echo "---targets---"; ls /c/bugBounty/targets 2>/dev/null
input.description: Locate cb-mpc source tree and pve_batch files

content.text: The finding references `src/cbmpc/protocol/pve_batch.cpp` but the actual file is `src/cbmpc/api/pve_batch_single_recipient.cpp` and the protocol implementation. Let me read the key files. Note the verifier notes reference paths like `pve_batch_single_recipient.cpp` for `decrypt_batch`, but the finding header says `src/cbmpc/protocol/pve_batch.cpp:183`. Let me check both.

This is very interesting. The actual code at `pve_batch.h:48-61` is **different** from what the finding describes. Let me analyze carefully.

The finding claims:
1. `convert(Q, L, b)` — a multi-arg convert that does `Q.clear()/resize(m)` making `Q.size()` attacker-controlled.
2. The pre-check `if (int(Q.size()) != n)` is a no-op.

Let me look at the converter to understand `converter.convert(Q, L, b)` semantics — specifically whether the std::vector overload truly resizes Q.

content.content: targets\cb-mpc\include-internal\cbmpc\internal\core\convert.h

content.text: The API path matches the finding's description. Now the critical question: does `converter.convert(Q, L, b)` actually `clear()` + `resize(m)` the `Q` vector to an attacker-controlled length `m`? Let me read the converter carefully, especially the std::vector overload and the deserialization semantics.

This confirms the core mechanics precisely:

1. **`convert.h:114-131`**: The `std::vector<T>` overload, on read (`!write`), does `value.clear()`, reads `count` via `convert_len`, checks only `count > MAX_CONTAINER_ELEMENTS (1<<20)`, then `value.resize(count)`. So `Q.size()` becomes attacker-controlled `m`, decoupled from member `n`. **Confirmed.**

2. **`pve_batch.h:48-52`**: The pre-check `if (int(Q.size()) != n)` runs while `Q` is still constructor-sized `n`, so it passes; then `converter.convert(Q, L, b)` overwrites `Q` to size `m`. **Confirmed no-op.**

3. **`convert.h:276-282`** `coinbase::convert(T&, mem_t)` does NOT call `deser()` (which would enforce no trailing bytes at line 261), so a short `Q` deserializes cleanly without strict trailing-byte rejection. **Confirmed** — the path uses `coinbase::convert`, not `deser`.

4. **`pve_batch.cpp:181-184`** `restore_from_decrypted` loops `for (int i=0;i<n;i++)` reading `Q[i]` while `Q.size()==m<n`. **Confirmed.**

Now I need to verify the two remaining load-bearing reachability claims: (a) `ecc_point_t::operator!=` / `operator==` dereferences the pointer for an OOB element, and (b) whether reaching `restore_from_decrypted` requires `base_pke.decrypt` to succeed and whether the OOB triggers. Let me check `base_ecc.cpp` and the base PKE, plus `vector_from_bin`.

content.content: targets\cb-mpc\src\cbmpc\crypto\base_ecc.cpp

content.text:
All load-bearing claims confirmed:

- **`operator==` (base_ecc.cpp:909-914)**: For an OOB-read `ecc_point_t`, `this->ptr` and `this->curve` come from out-of-bounds heap memory. If `ptr != nullptr` and `curve != nullptr` (likely for non-zeroed heap), it calls `curve.ptr->equ_points(*this, val)` — dereferencing a wild `curve.ptr` and the wild `EC_POINT*`. If the OOB bytes happen to be zero, `!ptr` returns early (no deref) but the loop still continues comparing — actually if `Q[i].ptr` is null, `operator==` returns `val.ptr==nullptr` which is false (val = x[i]*G is a real point), so `operator!=` returns true → returns `E_CRYPTO` cleanly. So the crash is heap-layout-dependent, exactly as the verifiers state (DoS certain in practice on non-zero adjacent heap; controlled OOB plausible).

- **`vector_from_bin` (base_bn.cpp:536-549)**: Strictly validates `n*size == mem.size` and resizes to `n`. So `x0` and `x1` are size `n` (safe). Only `Q` is short. **Confirmed** — only `Q` is the OOB vector.

Now let me confirm the base PKE is standard public-key crypto where the attacker (holding public `ek`) can forge a row that decrypts successfully, and check `genPVELabelWithPoint`. Also confirm `MAX_BATCH_COUNT=100000` and the c_api catch behavior. Let me also verify the scope boundary — that this is reachable from `include/cbmpc/api/`.

content.content:
1	#pragma once
2	
3	#include <vector>
4	
5	#include <cbmpc/api/curve.h>
6	#include <cbmpc/api/pve_base_pke.h>
7	#include <cbmpc/core/buf.h>
8	#include <cbmpc/core/error.h>
9	
10	namespace coinbase::api::pve {
11	
12	// ---------------------------------------------------------------------------
13	// Batch PVE (1P) API
14	// ---------------------------------------------------------------------------
15	//
16	// This API batches the core PVE algorithm for multiple scalars {x_i} in one
17	// ciphertext, sharing the same label and base PKE key.
18	//
19	// Notes:
20	// - `xs[i]` is interpreted as a big-endian integer and reduced modulo the curve order.
21	// - Defensive limit: each `xs[i].size` must be <= the curve order size in bytes.
22	// - `Qs_compressed[i]` is the compressed point encoding for the outer curve:
23	//   33 bytes for P-256/secp256k1, 32 bytes for Ed25519.
24	
25	error_t encrypt_batch(const base_pke_i& base_pke, curve_id curve, mem_t ek, mem_t label, const std::vector<mem_t>& xs,
26	                      buf_t& out_ciphertext);
27	
28	// Same as `encrypt_batch(...)` using `base_pke_default()`.
29	error_t encrypt_batch(curve_id curve, mem_t ek, mem_t label, const std::vector<mem_t>& xs, buf_t& out_ciphertext);
30	
31	error_t verify_batch(const base_pke_i& base_pke, curve_id curve, mem_t ek, mem_t ciphertext,
32	                     const std::vector<mem_t>& Qs_compressed, mem_t label);
33	
34	// Same as `verify_batch(...)` using `base_pke_default()`.
35	error_t verify_batch(curve_id curve, mem_t ek, mem_t ciphertext, const std::vector<mem_t>& Qs_compressed, mem_t label);
36	
37	// Decrypt a batch ciphertext, recovering the vector of scalars {x_i}.
38	//
39	// Output:
40	// - On success, `out_xs[i]` is a fixed-length big-endian encoding of x_i with
41	//   length equal to the curve order size in bytes (e.g., 32 bytes for P-256).
42	//
43	// Notes:
44	// - This function intentionally does not verify `ciphertext` before decryption.
45	//   Invalid ciphertexts may cause decryption to fail, but are designed to not
46	//   leak secret information.
47	// - If you need ciphertext validation, call `verify_batch(...)` first.
48	error_t decrypt_batch(const base_pke_i& base_pke, curve_id curve, mem_t dk, mem_t ek, mem_t ciphertext, mem_t label,
49	                      std::vector<buf_t>& out_xs);
50	
51	// Same as `decrypt_batch(...)` using `base_pke_default()`.
52	error_t decrypt_batch(curve_id curve, mem_t dk, mem_t ek, mem_t ciphertext, mem_t label, std::vector<buf_t>& out_xs);
53	
54	// Decrypt a batch ciphertext using an HSM-backed RSA private key.
55	error_t decrypt_batch_rsa_oaep_hsm(curve_id curve, mem_t dk_handle, mem_t ek, mem_t ciphertext, mem_t label,
56	                                   const rsa_oaep_hsm_decap_cb_t& cb, std::vector<buf_t>& out_xs);
57	
58	// Decrypt a batch ciphertext using an HSM-backed ECIES(P-256) private key.
59	error_t decrypt_batch_ecies_p256_hsm(curve_id curve, mem_t dk_handle, mem_t ek, mem_t ciphertext, mem_t label,
60	                                     const ecies_p256_hsm_ecdh_cb_t& cb, std::vector<buf_t>& out_xs);
61	
62	// Extract batch count from a batch ciphertext.
63	error_t get_batch_count(mem_t ciphertext, int& out_batch_count);
64	
65	// Extract the public values {Q_i} whose discrete logs are encrypted as the ciphertext, returning compressed point
66	// encodings.
67	error_t get_public_keys_compressed_batch(mem_t ciphertext, std::vector<buf_t>& out_Qs_compressed);
68	
69	// Extract the associated label from a batch ciphertext.
70	error_t get_Label_batch(mem_t ciphertext, buf_t& out_label);
71	
72	}  // namespace coinbase::api::pve
73

content.text:
This is a clean confirmation. Key observations:

1. **Public API entry confirmed**: `coinbase::api::pve::decrypt_batch` is in `include/cbmpc/api/pve_batch_single_recipient.h` — squarely in scope.

2. **The header doc (lines 43-47)** explicitly says: "This function intentionally does not verify `ciphertext` before decryption. Invalid ciphertexts may cause decryption to fail, but are designed to not leak secret information." This is the *intended-design disclaimer*. The verifiers correctly note it disclaims **secret leakage only**, not memory safety. An OOB read / wild-pointer deref is **outside** that disclaimer. This strengthens the finding — the author explicitly anticipated adversarial ciphertexts on this path but only reasoned about confidentiality, not memory safety.

3. **Base PKE is standard KEM/DEM public-key crypto** (`kem_pve_base_pke_t`): the attacker holds the public `ek` and can encrypt chosen plaintexts that decrypt back correctly. `genPVELabelWithPoint(label, Q)` (line 253-256) is `sha256(serialized Q)` — fully attacker-computable. **Confirmed** the attacker can forge a row whose `base_pke.decrypt` succeeds.

Now, one subtle point I want to validate independently — whether the OOB read is reachable **without** needing the equality `Q[i] == x[i]*G` to hold (the easiest crash). The loop is:
```cpp
for (int i = 0; i < n; i++) {
  MODULO(q) x[i] = x0[i] + x1[i];
  if (Q[i] != x[i] * G) return E_CRYPTO;   // reads Q[i]
}
```
With `m=0` (empty inner Q), `Q.size()==0`, so the very first iteration `i=0` reads `Q[0]` — immediate OOB on a zero-size vector. But wait — does the curve loop at `pve_batch_single_recipient.cpp:165-167` (`for (const auto& q : pve_ct.get_Qs())`) reject an empty Q? No, it iterates zero times for empty Q, passing trivially. And `restore_from_decrypted` is only reached if `base_pke.decrypt` succeeds for some row. Let me confirm there's no other guard that requires `Q` non-empty before decrypt. 

Let me also double check the `MODULO`/`x[i]` indexing: `x` is `xs`, resized to `n` in `decrypt` (line 192), so `x[i]` is safe; `x0`,`x1` are size `n`. Only `Q[i]` is OOB. Confirmed.

Let me verify the c_api wrapper's catch behavior (relevant to whether a SIGSEGV is caught — it isn't, but a C++ OOB read doesn't throw anyway) and check `MAX_CIPHERTEXT_BLOB_SIZE` to ensure a large-`n` blob fits within size validation.

content.content: No matches found

content.text: The `MAX_*` constants are referenced via `coinbase::api::detail::` namespace. Let me find their definitions to confirm a large-`n` blob (with only m=0 inner Q) fits within `MAX_CIPHERTEXT_BLOB_SIZE`. This matters because if the blob with n=100000 requires fixed-size content proportional to n, it might exceed the size limit. But critically, the blob's *serialized size* is determined by the inner `ct` content (which has m Q points + 128 rows), NOT by the outer `batch_count` field (a single uint32). So a blob with `batch_count=100000` but only m=0 or m=1 inner Q is tiny. Let me confirm the size limits and that they're large enough for the 128 rows.

content.content: No matches found

content.text:
`MAX_CIPHERTEXT_BLOB_SIZE = 64 MiB` — far larger than needed. A blob with `batch_count=100000` but inner Q of length m=0 or m=1 plus the 128 fixed rows is on the order of kilobytes. The size validation poses no obstacle. **Confirmed.**

I now have full confirmation of every load-bearing claim. Let me do one final check on the `git log` to confirm I'm looking at the right upstream commit, and confirm there's no `_GLIBCXX_ASSERTIONS` hardening that would turn `operator[]` into a checked access (which would convert the OOB into a clean abort rather than a wild deref — still a DoS either way, but affects the "controlled OOB" upside).

input.description: Confirm upstream commit, remote, and compile hardening flags

content.content:
e97eec7 fix: bound modulus buffers and OpenSSL install paths (#117)
---remote---
origin	https://github.com/coinbase/cb-mpc.git (fetch)
origin	https://github.com/coinbase/cb-mpc.git (push)
---hardening---
cmake/compilation_flags.cmake:29:  set_cxx_flags("-D_FORTIFY_SOURCE=2")
cmake/compilation_flags.cmake:57:if(CMAKE_CXX_FLAGS MATCHES "-fsanitize=" OR CMAKE_C_FLAGS MATCHES "-fsanitize=" OR
cmake/compilation_flags.cmake:58:   CMAKE_EXE_LINKER_FLAGS MATCHES "-fsanitize=" OR CMAKE_SHARED_LINKER_FLAGS MATCHES "-fsanitize=")

content.text:
Confirmed:
- Upstream `coinbase/cb-mpc` at commit `e97eec7`, matching the third verifier's referenced commit.
- Hardening: only `-D_FORTIFY_SOURCE=2`, which does **not** instrument `std::vector::operator[]`. No `_GLIBCXX_ASSERTIONS` or libc++ hardening. So the OOB read on `Q[i]` is a genuine unchecked access reading adjacent heap as an `ecc_point_t`, exactly as claimed. The wild-pointer deref path in `operator==` is real (in default release builds).

Every load-bearing claim in the finding is verified against the real code:

1. **Decoupling**: `convert.h:114-131` `std::vector` overload does `clear()`/`resize(m)` with attacker-controlled `m` (≤ 2^20). Member `n` stays = `batch_count`. ✓
2. **No-op pre-check**: `pve_batch.h:48-52` checks `Q.size()!=n` before the resize, while `Q` is still ctor-sized. ✓
3. **Non-strict deser**: `coinbase::convert(T&, mem_t)` (convert.h:276-282) does NOT enforce trailing-byte rejection (unlike `deser`). ✓
4. **verify() bypass**: only `verify()` (pve_batch.cpp:78) enforces `Q.size()==n`; decrypt path uses `skip_verify=true`. ✓
5. **OOB loop**: `restore_from_decrypted` (pve_batch.cpp:181-184) reads `Q[i]` for `i in [0,n)` while `Q.size()==m<n`; `x0`/`x1`/`xs` are all safely size `n`. ✓
6. **Wild deref**: `ecc_point_t::operator==` (base_ecc.cpp:909-914) derefs OOB `curve.ptr`/`EC_POINT*`. ✓
7. **Reachability**: base PKE is standard public-key KEM/DEM; attacker holds public `ek`, computes `inner_label`, forges a decrypting row. With m=0 the OOB hits on `Q[0]` immediately. ✓
8. **Scope**: entry is `coinbase::api::pve::decrypt_batch` in `include/cbmpc/api/`. ✓
9. **Size limits**: `MAX_CIPHERTEXT_BLOB_SIZE=64MiB` is no obstacle (blob is tiny). ✓
10. **Intended-design disclaimer (header lines 43-47) only covers secret leakage, not memory safety** — OOB is outside the disclaimer. ✓

The only candidate overstatement (correctly flagged by 2 of 3 verifiers) is that `get_public_keys_compressed_batch`/`get_Label_batch` are NOT OOB-vulnerable — they iterate the m-sized `get_Qs()` directly and never index to `n`. Only the `decrypt_batch` family hits the n-bounded loop. This does not affect the core finding.

Now let me produce the bounty-aware ranked report.

input.executive_summary:
One finding survived verification against pristine upstream coinbase/cb-mpc at commit e97eec7 (origin github.com/coinbase/cb-mpc, clean tree). It is REAL, REACHABLE through an in-scope public API, and NOT covered by the existing design disclaimer. It is a memory-safety bug (OOB std::vector read + wild EC_POINT* / curve-pointer dereference), not a key-compromise bug.

Core: in the batch-PVE decrypt path, the outer batch_count (n, the std::vector loop bound) is decoupled from the inner length-prefixed Q vector (size m) and is never re-validated on decrypt. A malicious counterparty supplies a serialized batch-PVE ciphertext blob whose outer batch_count is n (e.g. up to MAX_BATCH_COUNT=100000) but whose inner Q vector deserializes to m < n (e.g. m=0 or m=1). decrypt_batch runs with skip_verify=true, so the only Q.size()==n check (in verify(), pve_batch.cpp:78) is bypassed, and restore_from_decrypted (pve_batch.cpp:181-184) loops i in [0,n) reading Q[i] while Q.size()==m, an unchecked operator[] OOB read of a std::vector<ecc_point_t>. The OOB element's bytes are then consumed by ecc_point_t::operator!= -> operator== (base_ecc.cpp:909-914), which dereferences the out-of-bounds curve.ptr and EC_POINT*.

I verified every load-bearing link: the std::vector deserialization overload (convert.h:114-131) clear()+resize(m) with attacker-controlled m bounded only by MAX_CONTAINER_ELEMENTS=2^20; the pre-check at pve_batch.h:48-52 is a genuine no-op (runs while Q is still ctor-sized n); coinbase::convert(T&,mem_t) (convert.h:276-282) does NOT enforce trailing-byte rejection unlike deser(); x0/x1/xs are all safely size n via vector_from_bin (base_bn.cpp:536-549) so only Q is short; base PKE is standard KEM/DEM public-key crypto (pve_base.h:50-82) so the attacker holding the public ek can forge a row whose base_pke.decrypt SUCCEEDS and reach restore_from_decrypted; and release builds carry only -D_FORTIFY_SOURCE=2 (no _GLIBCXX_ASSERTIONS / libc++ hardening, cmake/compilation_flags.cmake:29) so operator[] is genuinely unchecked. The header doc (pve_batch_single_recipient.h:43-47) discloses only that invalid ciphertexts will not leak secrets; it does not disclaim memory safety, so this is outside intended behavior.

Tier assessment: this is reachable purely through the public API boundary by a malicious party who only crosses the serialized-ciphertext interface, with >=1 honest party (the decryptor) running unmodified code. Impact is a remotely-triggerable crash/DoS of the honest decryptor, with a plausible-but-unproven controlled-OOB-read upside depending on heap layout. No honest-party secret is disclosed and there is no demonstrated key compromise or RCE. I map it to Medium ($2k), conservatively, with a clear note that if an end-to-end PoC demonstrates only an unreliable/hard-to-control crash on a process that is not a persistent service, a triage team could argue Low; and that High would require demonstrating attacker-controlled OOB content flow (e.g. info-leak or control-flow influence), which is not established here.

The one candidate overstatement (that get_public_keys_compressed_batch / get_Label_batch are equally vulnerable) is refuted: those iterate the m-sized get_Qs() directly and never index to n, so they are not OOB. Only the decrypt_batch family (including the two HSM variants that delegate to it) is exploitable. This does not weaken the core finding.

ranked_findings.title: Batch-PVE decrypt: inner Q vector length not re-checked against batch_count on the skip_verify path -> OOB std::vector<ecc_point_t> read + wild EC_POINT*/curve-pointer dereference on the honest decryptor
ranked_findings.vuln_class: Out-of-bounds read on deserialized container (missing length re-validation) leading to wild-pointer dereference (CWE-125 / CWE-822)
ranked_findings.public_api_entry: coinbase::api::pve::decrypt_batch(curve_id, dk, ek, ciphertext, label, out_xs) in include/cbmpc/api/pve_batch_single_recipient.h (also reachable via decrypt_batch_rsa_oaep_hsm and decrypt_batch_ecies_p256_hsm, which delegate to the same function)
ranked_findings.why: Verified end-to-end against upstream cb-mpc @ e97eec7. (1) Decoupling: convert.h std::vector<T> overload (lines 114-131) on read does value.clear() then value.resize(count) where count is the attacker's wire length-prefix, bounded only by MAX_CONTAINER_ELEMENTS=1<<20; so Q.size() becomes attacker-controlled m, independent of the member n. (2) Pre-check is a no-op: ec_pve_batch_t::convert (include-internal/.../pve_batch.h:48-52) tests `if(int(Q.size())!=n)` while Q is still constructor-sized n (pve_batch.h:13 Q.resize(n)), so it passes, THEN converter.convert(Q,L,b) shrinks Q to m. (3) Non-strict deser: decrypt_batch uses coinbase::convert(pve_ct, blob.ct) (convert.h:276-282) which, unlike deser() (convert.h:254-266), does NOT reject trailing bytes, so a short Q deserializes cleanly. (4) verify() bypass: the only Q.size()==n enforcement is verify() (pve_batch.cpp:78), and decrypt_batch calls decrypt(...,skip_verify=true) (pve_batch_single_recipient.cpp:173-174), so verify is short-circuited (pve_batch.cpp:193). The post-deser curve loop (pve_batch_single_recipient.cpp:165-167) only iterates the m present get_Qs() entries, so it cannot detect m<n. restore_from_decrypted (pve_batch.cpp:144-187) validates only row_index and buffer sizes, never Q.size(). (5) OOB: restore_from_decrypted loops `for(int i=0;i<n;i++){ x[i]=x0[i]+x1[i]; if(Q[i]!=x[i]*G)...}` (181-184) reading Q[i] up to n-1 while Q.size()==m; x0/x1 are size n (vector_from_bin, base_bn.cpp:536-549, strictly checks n*size==mem.size and resizes to n), xs is resized to n in decrypt (pve_batch.cpp:192) -- so ONLY Q is short. Q[i] is std::vector::operator[], unchecked: cmake/compilation_flags.cmake carries only -D_FORTIFY_SOURCE=2 (no _GLIBCXX_ASSERTIONS / libc++ hardening), so operator[] is not bounds-checked. (6) Deref: the OOB element interpreted as ecc_point_t{ecurve_t curve; EC_POINT* ptr} flows into operator!= -> operator== (base_ecc.cpp:909-914): if the OOB-read ptr and curve bytes are non-null it calls curve.ptr->equ_points(...), dereferencing a wild EC_POINT*/curve pointer. (7) Reachability: base PKE is a standard KEM/DEM public-key scheme (pve_base.h:50-82, RSA-OAEP-2048 / ECIES-P256); the attacker holds the PUBLIC ek and controls Q,label so computes inner_label=genPVELabelWithPoint(label,Q)=label+'-'+hex(sha256(Q)) (pve_base.h:253-256) and forges a row.c that base_pke.decrypt accepts -> restore_from_decrypted is entered. With m=0 the OOB hits on the very first iteration Q[0] regardless of equality. Scope: entry is coinbase::api::pve::decrypt_batch in include/cbmpc/api/ (in scope); demo-* and c_api/* not relied on as entry. Intended-behavior check: the header doc (pve_batch_single_recipient.h:43-47) disclaims only secret-leakage on invalid ciphertexts, NOT memory safety, so the OOB is out of intended scope. Confidence is high but not 1.0 because no end-to-end PoC blob has been built here: the attacker must assemble a fully self-consistent serialized ec_pve_batch_t (correct length prefix m for Q, valid L/b, and 128 row_t entries with at least one row.c a real RSA/ECIES KEM-DEM ciphertext under ek that decrypts to a buffer of the size restore_from_decrypted expects). All inputs are attacker-constructible, but the crash's reliability and any controlled-OOB upside depend on heap layout.
ranked_findings.suggested_next_step: Build the minimal independent-parties PoC (below) and run the honest decryptor under AddressSanitizer to capture the exact OOB read on Q and the subsequent wild deref in ecc_point_t::operator==. Then report to Coinbase with the ASan trace. Recommended fix to suggest: in ec_pve_batch_t::convert (post-deserialization) or at the top of decrypt()/restore_from_decrypted(), enforce `if (int(Q.size()) != n) return E_BADARG/set_error()` AFTER reading Q (the existing pre-read check is structurally a no-op); equivalently add a size re-check in decrypt_batch right after coinbase::convert(pve_ct, blob.ct) before any use of get_Qs().

recommended_priority_order: Reproduce finding #1 end-to-end via include/cbmpc/api/ only: (1) honest party generates (ek,dk) and publishes ek; (2) attacker calls encrypt_batch(curve, ek, label, xs) with a single scalar (m=1) to obtain a well-formed pve_batch_ciphertext_blob_v1_t; (3) attacker re-serializes a tampered blob keeping the inner ct (Q length-prefix = 1 or 0, valid 128 rows) but setting the OUTER batch_count field to a large n (e.g. 50000); (4) honest party runs unmodified decrypt_batch(curve, dk, ek, tampered_blob, label, out_xs). Run the honest side under ASan to capture the Q[i] OOB read and the wild deref. Easiest crash variant: craft m=0 so Q[0] is OOB on the first loop iteration as soon as any row decrypts.

Confirm crash reliability and whether the OOB content is attacker-influenceable (heap-grooming) to decide between Medium and a possible High up-tier; if only an unreliable crash on a short-lived process is shown, pre-empt a Low down-tier argument with a service-context note.

Submit to Coinbase cb-mpc bounty with the ASan trace, the exact code citations, and the suggested one-line fix (re-validate Q.size()==n after deserialization / before the n-bounded loop). Explicitly scope OUT the get_public_keys_compressed_batch / get_Label_batch paths as non-vulnerable to avoid an over-claim that triage could use to discount the report.
input.coverage_and_caveats:
SCOPE OF THIS REVIEW: I verified the single supplied finding against the real source at C:\bugBounty\targets\cb-mpc on upstream coinbase/cb-mpc commit e97eec7 (git remote origin = github.com/coinbase/cb-mpc). I did NOT do an independent sweep for other bugs; this report covers only the provided candidate.

NOTE ON FILE/LINE: The finding header cites src/cbmpc/protocol/pve_batch.cpp:183, which is correct in this tree (the OOB read `if (Q[i] != x[i]*G)` is at pve_batch.cpp:183, inside restore_from_decrypted). The public-API plumbing lives in src/cbmpc/api/pve_batch_single_recipient.cpp and the deserialization mechanics in include-internal/cbmpc/internal/core/convert.h and include-internal/cbmpc/internal/protocol/pve_batch.h. All four files were read in full for the load-bearing lines.

WHAT IS SOLID (directly confirmed in code): the n-vs-m decoupling; the no-op pre-check; the non-strict coinbase::convert vs strict deser distinction; the verify() bypass on skip_verify=true; x0/x1/xs all safely sized n so only Q is OOB; operator[] is unchecked under the project's actual compile flags (-D_FORTIFY_SOURCE=2 only); operator== dereferences the OOB element's curve/EC_POINT* pointers; base PKE is a standard public-key KEM/DEM so a malicious holder of the public ek can forge a decrypting row; MAX_CIPHERTEXT_BLOB_SIZE=64MiB (mem_util.h:17) is not an obstacle since a tampered blob with large outer batch_count but tiny inner Q is only a few KB; the public API entry is in include/cbmpc/api/ (in scope) and the honest party runs unmodified code with the attacker only crossing the serialized-ciphertext boundary.

DEEPEST UNCERTAINTY (why confidence is 0.88, not higher, and why poc_required=true for this Medium): (1) No end-to-end serialized blob has been constructed and executed here. The exact wire layout of ec_pve_batch_t::convert is: length-prefixed Q (m points) then L then b (buf128_t), then for each of kappa=128 rows the triple (x_bin, r, c). A working PoC must produce a self-consistent blob where at least one row.c is a genuine RSA-OAEP-2048 or ECIES-P256 KEM-DEM ciphertext under the published ek with inner_label=label+'-'+hex(sha256(serialized m-point Q)), and whose decrypted buffer satisfies restore_from_decrypted's size gate for that row's bi bit (either r01.size==16 for bi=1, or x1_bin.size==n*curve_size for bi=0). The m=0 / m=1 crash path is reachable, but I have not byte-assembled and run it, so the harness-level reproducibility is asserted from code reading, not demonstrated. (2) Severity sensitivity: the certain outcome is a crash/DoS of the honest decryptor; the 'controlled OOB read' upside is heap-layout dependent and UNPROVEN. If a PoC shows only a non-deterministic crash (e.g. the OOB ecc_point_t happens to read zeroed memory -> operator== returns early via `if(!ptr)` with no deref, yielding a clean E_CRYPTO rather than a fault on some heap states), a triage team could argue the bug is intermittently triggerable; that is why I held to Medium rather than High and flagged a possible Low down-tier risk. (3) Process-context dependence: bounty value of a DoS depends on whether the honest decryptor runs as a long-lived service vs a one-shot CLI; the repo alone does not settle that. (4) The candidate's claim that get_public_keys_compressed_batch / get_Label_batch are equally vulnerable is REFUTED here (they iterate the m-sized get_Qs() and never index to n); reporting them as vulnerable would be an over-claim and is excluded. NULL-RESULT NOTE: no key-compromise, key-recovery, RCE, or secret-leakage path was found in or around this finding; the impact ceiling on current evidence is memory-safety/DoS, hence the Medium ($2k) mapping rather than High/Critical.

content.content: Structured output provided successfully

content.text:
I have completed the verification and produced the bounty-aware ranked report via the StructuredOutput tool.

Summary of what I did and found:

I verified the single supplied finding against the real cb-mpc source at `C:\bugBounty\targets\cb-mpc` (upstream `coinbase/cb-mpc` @ commit `e97eec7`). Every load-bearing claim holds:

- `convert.h:114-131` — the `std::vector` deserialization overload does `clear()`+`resize(m)` with attacker-controlled `m` (≤ 2^20).
- `pve_batch.h:48-52` — the `Q.size()!=n` pre-check is a genuine no-op (runs while `Q` is still constructor-sized).
- `convert.h:276-282` — `coinbase::convert(T&,mem_t)` does NOT reject trailing bytes (unlike `deser`).
- `pve_batch.cpp:78/193` — the only size enforcement is in `verify()`, bypassed by `skip_verify=true`.
- `pve_batch.cpp:181-184` — `restore_from_decrypted` reads `Q[i]` for `i in [0,n)` while `Q.size()==m`; `x0/x1/xs` are all safely size `n`, so only `Q` is OOB.
- `base_ecc.cpp:909-914` — `operator==` dereferences the OOB element's wild `curve.ptr`/`EC_POINT*`.
- `cmake/compilation_flags.cmake:29` — only `-D_FORTIFY_SOURCE=2`, so `operator[]` is genuinely unchecked.
- Base PKE is standard public-key KEM/DEM, so the attacker holding the public `ek` can forge a decrypting row and reach the OOB loop.

Tier mapping: **Medium / $2,000** (conservative). It is a remotely-triggerable OOB read + wild-pointer deref (DoS) on the honest decryptor, reachable purely through the in-scope public API `coinbase::api::pve::decrypt_batch`, outside the header's secret-leakage-only disclaimer. No key compromise or RCE, so not High/Critical; `poc_required=true` with the minimal independent-parties PoC described.

I also recorded the one refuted over-claim (the `get_public_keys_compressed_batch` / `get_Label_batch` paths are NOT OOB) and the deepest-uncertainty spots (no end-to-end blob assembled; the controlled-OOB upside is heap-layout-dependent and unproven; severity sensitivity to crash reliability and process context) in `coverage_and_caveats`.

Relevant files (absolute paths):
- `C:\bugBounty\targets\cb-mpc\src\cbmpc\protocol\pve_batch.cpp`
- `C:\bugBounty\targets\cb-mpc\src\cbmpc\api\pve_batch_single_recipient.cpp`
- `C:\bugBounty\targets\cb-mpc\include-internal\cbmpc\internal\protocol\pve_batch.h`
- `C:\bugBounty\targets\cb-mpc\include-internal\cbmpc\internal\core\convert.h`
- `C:\bugBounty\targets\cb-mpc\src\cbmpc\crypto\base_ecc.cpp`
- `C:\bugBounty\targets\cb-mpc\src\cbmpc\crypto\base_bn.cpp`
- `C:\bugBounty\targets\cb-mpc\include-internal\cbmpc\internal\protocol\pve_base.h`
- `C:\bugBounty\targets\cb-mpc\include\cbmpc\api\pve_batch_single_recipient.h`
- `C:\bugBounty\targets\cb-mpc\src\cbmpc\api\mem_util.h`
